title: Midwifery Knowledge Challenge
seoDescription: Take a fun 80-question midwifery knowledge quiz covering newborn basics, parent support, safety checks, communication, and practical judgement.
pageTitle: Only 7% Pass This Midwifery Knowledge Challenge. Can You?
summary: A fun staged midwifery knowledge quiz about parent support, newborn basics, safety checks, communication, and calm judgement.

homepage.title: Can You Pass This Midwifery Knowledge Challenge?
homepage.summary: Think you can support calm decisions through 10 birth-care inspired rounds?

footer.aboutText: This midwifery knowledge test is a staged quiz inspired by broad skills often linked with maternity and newborn support: listening, observation, warmth, privacy, communication, parent-centred care, and getting extra help when needed. Midwifery practice can vary by country and setting, but clear judgement and respectful support are widely valued. This quiz is for entertainment and curiosity only. It is not medical advice, not an emergency guide, and not an official midwifery qualification or clinical exam.

landing.quickStartText: The first checks feel gentle. The later rounds need calm birth-care judgement.

profiles.title: You stayed calm through the hardest scenarios.
profiles.copy: Excellent judgement across the full challenge. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Strong midwifery-style thinking.
profiles.copy: You handled parent and baby care questions with confidence. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Good practical score.
profiles.copy: You showed steady reasoning across several care rounds. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Keep sharpening the checks.
profiles.copy: The later rounds reward respectful communication, safe priorities, and careful observation. Your best area was {stage}.

stageGroups.title: Birth Care Warm Up
stageGroups.encouragement: Good start. The next round asks for closer attention to parent and baby care.

questions.prompt: What is most useful at the start of supportive care?
questions.explanation: Listening helps care respond to the person’s needs.

questions.prompt: A bag has 3 clean towels and 2 blankets. How many items are there?
questions.explanation: 3 plus 2 equals 5.

questions.prompt: Which item is most linked with checking a baby’s weight?
questions.explanation: A scale is used to check weight.

questions.prompt: If a newborn must be kept warm, which action best supports that?
choices: Dry and cover the baby appropriately
Place the baby in a cold room
questions.explanation: Drying and covering can help maintain warmth.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 2, 4, 6, 8, ?
questions.explanation: The sequence increases by 2 each time.

questions.prompt: Which choice best shows respectful maternity care?
questions.explanation: Respectful care includes explanation and consent.

questions.prompt: Which sign would need careful attention?
questions.explanation: Sudden heavy bleeding can be urgent.

questions.prompt: A visit begins at 09:00 and lasts 30 minutes. When does it end?
questions.explanation: Thirty minutes after 09:00 is 09:30.

stageGroups.title: Newborn Basics
stageGroups.encouragement: Nice work. Now the quiz moves into simple newborn checks.

questions.prompt: Why is warmth important for a newborn?
questions.explanation: Newborns can lose heat quickly and need warmth.

questions.prompt: A baby has 4 baby changes recorded in the morning and 3 later. What is the total?
questions.explanation: 4 plus 3 equals 7.

questions.prompt: Which observation is most specific?
questions.explanation: A feeding time is a specific observation.

questions.prompt: If every baby in a list needs an identity band checked, and Baby A is in the list, what follows?
choices: Baby A needs the band checked
questions.explanation: The rule applies to every baby in the list.

questions.prompt: Which pattern fits regular checks every hour?
questions.explanation: Hourly checks are one hour apart.

questions.prompt: A parent says the baby is feeding differently today. What is the best response?
questions.explanation: A parent’s observation can be important and should be assessed.

questions.prompt: Which item is most linked with feeding support?
questions.explanation: Positioning advice can support feeding.

questions.prompt: A baby sleeps for 2 hours, wakes for 1 hour, then sleeps for 2 more. How much sleep is that?
questions.explanation: Only the two sleep periods count, so 2 plus 2 equals 4.

stageGroups.title: Parent Support
stageGroups.encouragement: You are building pace. The next round adds practical judgement.

questions.prompt: Which response best supports an anxious parent?
choices: Listen calmly and explain next steps
questions.explanation: Calm listening and clear explanation can reduce anxiety.

questions.prompt: A parent drinks 250 ml, then 200 ml. What is the total?
questions.explanation: 250 plus 200 equals 450 ml.

questions.prompt: Which statement is clearest?
choices: Your blood pressure will be checked again in 30 minutes
questions.explanation: It gives a specific action and time.

questions.prompt: If all concerns about reduced movement need reporting, and this is such a concern, what follows?
questions.explanation: The rule says this type of concern should be reported.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is safest for a concern?
choices: Listen, assess, record, report if needed
questions.explanation: A safe response starts by listening and assessing.

questions.prompt: Why should explanations avoid jargon?
questions.explanation: Plain language helps people understand care decisions.

questions.prompt: Which detail is most relevant to feeding support?
questions.explanation: Feeding frequency is relevant to feeding support.

questions.prompt: A check happens every 20 minutes for one hour. How many checks after minute 0?
questions.explanation: After minute 0, checks occur at 20, 40, and 60 minutes.

stageGroups.title: Safe Birth Thinking
stageGroups.encouragement: Strong support thinking. Now the questions focus on safe priorities.

questions.prompt: Which situation usually needs the quickest attention?
questions.explanation: Heavy bleeding may be urgent.

questions.prompt: A birth note has 3 checks completed and 2 left. How many checks are listed?
questions.explanation: 3 completed plus 2 left equals 5.

questions.prompt: Which action best protects dignity?
choices: Cover the person appropriately and explain care
questions.explanation: Privacy, covering, and explanation support dignity.

questions.prompt: A pattern is breathe, rest, sip, breathe, rest, ?. What comes next?
questions.explanation: The pattern repeats breathe, rest, sip.

questions.prompt: Which information is most useful in a care update?
questions.explanation: A change in condition can be important for ongoing care.

questions.prompt: If a plan is not understood, what is the best next step?
questions.explanation: Clear explanation supports informed decisions.

questions.prompt: Which statement is an observation?
questions.explanation: A duration is a specific observation.

questions.prompt: A person walks 10 units, rests, then walks 15 units. How far did they walk?
questions.explanation: 10 plus 15 equals 25 units.

stageGroups.title: After-Birth Checks
stageGroups.encouragement: Good progress. The next stage asks for sharper observation and comparison.

questions.prompt: Why are after-birth checks repeated?
questions.explanation: Repeated checks help identify changes.

questions.prompt: A temperature changes from 36.8 to 37.3. What is the increase?
questions.explanation: 37.3 minus 36.8 equals 0.5.

questions.prompt: Which concern is most relevant after birth?
questions.explanation: Increasing pain with new bleeding may need assessment.

questions.prompt: If all new symptoms must be reviewed and this is a new symptom, what follows?
questions.explanation: The rule says new symptoms must be reviewed.

questions.prompt: Which pattern shows steady increase?
questions.explanation: 1, 2, 3, 4 increases steadily.

questions.prompt: A parent is unsure about a routine check. What is most helpful?
choices: Explain purpose and ask for questions
questions.explanation: Explanation and questions support understanding.

questions.prompt: Which comparison is fairest?
choices: Same measurement method at different times
questions.explanation: The same method makes comparison more reliable.

questions.prompt: A baby gains 30 g on one day and 40 g the next. What is the total gain?
questions.explanation: 30 plus 40 equals 70 grams.

stageGroups.title: Communication Round
stageGroups.encouragement: Clear communication is doing a lot of work here. Next comes pressure reasoning.

questions.prompt: Which care update message is clearest?
choices: The parent reported dizziness when standing
questions.explanation: It states the person and concern clearly.

questions.prompt: Why should choices be explained before non-urgent care?
choices: People should understand and be involved
questions.explanation: Explanation helps people take part in care decisions.

questions.prompt: If a baby must be fed at least every 3 hours, what is the longest planned gap?
questions.explanation: At least every 3 hours means the planned gap should not exceed 3 hours.

questions.prompt: A shift has 6 checks done and 4 still needed. How many checks are there in total?
questions.explanation: 6 plus 4 equals 10.

questions.prompt: Which order best supports a safe update?
choices: No name, no time, no plan
questions.explanation: A useful update identifies who, what, and what is needed.

questions.prompt: A parent’s preference conflicts with a routine plan but safety is not immediate. What is best?
choices: Discuss options and respect informed choice
questions.explanation: Informed choice matters when there is time to discuss options.

questions.prompt: Which statement best separates fact and interpretation?
choices: Fact: pulse counted as 92; interpretation: may need review
Fact: the room is nice; interpretation: all is safe
Fact: someone thinks; interpretation: no care needed
Fact: it feels busy; interpretation: records are complete
questions.explanation: A counted pulse is a fact; whether it needs review is interpretation.

questions.prompt: A record has checks at 08:00, 10:00, 12:00. What time comes next if the pattern continues?
questions.explanation: The checks are 2 hours apart, so 14:00 is next.

stageGroups.title: Risk Awareness
stageGroups.encouragement: You are into the harder rounds now. Stay calm and read carefully.

questions.prompt: A small concern appears along with a major warning sign. What should guide action?
questions.explanation: Major warning signs should guide urgent action.

questions.prompt: A care task takes 18 minutes. Two similar tasks take how long?
questions.explanation: 2 times 18 equals 36 minutes.

questions.prompt: Which detail is least useful for clinical priority?
questions.explanation: Favourite colour does not guide clinical priority.

questions.prompt: If all urgent concerns need extra help and this concern is urgent, what follows?
choices: It is no longer a concern
questions.explanation: The rule says urgent concerns need extra help.

questions.prompt: A sequence is 5, 10, 20, 40. What comes next?
questions.explanation: Each number is doubled.

questions.prompt: A planned home visit feels convenient, but the person now reports a new danger sign. What is the hidden risk?
choices: Convenience may distract from changing need
Danger signs can be ignored at home
questions.explanation: New risk information should change the plan when needed.

questions.prompt: Which conclusion is safest from one incomplete record?
choices: It needs checking before strong conclusions
It should replace all other evidence
questions.explanation: Incomplete records should be checked before strong conclusions.

questions.prompt: A baby has 2 feeds, then 3 feeds, then 4 feeds over equal periods. If the pattern continues, what comes next?
questions.explanation: The count increases by 1 each time.

stageGroups.title: Judgement Challenge
stageGroups.encouragement: Excellent progress. The final round asks for careful, balanced decisions.

questions.prompt: A parent improves after extra support, but also had more rest. What is the main reasoning issue?
choices: More than one factor may explain improvement
questions.explanation: Both support and rest may have contributed.

questions.prompt: A baby weighs 3.2 kg and later 3.4 kg. What is the gain?
questions.explanation: 3.4 minus 3.2 equals 0.2 kg.

questions.prompt: Which assumption is unsafe?
questions.explanation: A person may not understand even if they do not ask a question.

questions.prompt: All high-risk cases need review by a more experienced clinician. This case is high-risk. What must happen?
choices: Review by a more experienced clinician is needed
Only a routine note is allowed
questions.explanation: The rule says high-risk cases need review by a more experienced clinician.

questions.prompt: A pattern uses add 3, then double. Starting at 2 gives 5, 10, 13, 26. What comes next?
questions.explanation: After doubling to 26, the next step is add 3.

questions.prompt: Which comparison is strongest?
questions.explanation: Consistent observations over time are stronger evidence.

questions.prompt: A person has 50 minutes before transport. One check takes 12 minutes and another takes 18 minutes. How much time remains?
questions.explanation: 12 plus 18 equals 30, and 50 minus 30 leaves 20 minutes.

questions.prompt: Which sign should not be dismissed just because the person looks calm?
questions.explanation: Severe pain should be assessed even if the person appears calm.

stageGroups.title: Continuity of Care
stageGroups.encouragement: Great work. One final challenge remains, with careful choices across parent and newborn care.

questions.prompt: A parent saw two different team members and received slightly different advice. What is the best next step?
choices: Clarify the plan and give one consistent explanation
questions.explanation: Consistent, clarified advice helps people understand the care plan.

questions.prompt: A newborn check is due every 4 hours. How many checks are due in 12 hours?
questions.explanation: 12 divided by 4 equals 3 checks.

questions.prompt: Which note is most useful for the next caregiver?
choices: Fed for 20 minutes and settled after support
questions.explanation: It gives specific information about feeding and response.

questions.prompt: If every unresolved concern needs follow-up and this concern is unresolved, what follows?
questions.explanation: The rule says unresolved concerns need follow-up.

questions.prompt: A pattern is check, support, record, check, support, ?. What comes next?
questions.explanation: The pattern repeats check, support, record.

questions.prompt: A parent understands the plan but is still worried. What is the strongest response?
choices: Acknowledge the worry and explain what to watch for
questions.explanation: Acknowledging worry and giving clear next steps supports confidence.

questions.prompt: Which detail best connects care across shifts?
choices: A clear record of what changed and what was done
questions.explanation: Clear records help the next team understand changes and actions.

questions.prompt: A parent rests for 45 minutes, then 30 minutes. How much rest is that total?
questions.explanation: 45 plus 30 equals 75 minutes.

stageGroups.title: Final Midwifery Challenge
stageGroups.encouragement: You reached the final round. These questions reward safe, respectful judgement.

questions.prompt: A newborn is cold and another routine form is incomplete. What should come first?
questions.explanation: A newborn’s warmth is more immediate than a routine form.

questions.prompt: A parent drinks 300 ml, 250 ml, and 150 ml. What is the total?
questions.explanation: 300 plus 250 plus 150 equals 700 ml.

questions.prompt: Which statement best shows respectful professional judgement?
choices: I will explain options and listen before deciding
I will assume every person wants the same thing
questions.explanation: Respectful judgement includes explanation, listening, and evidence.

questions.prompt: A pattern is 3, 6, 9, 12, ?. What comes next?
questions.explanation: The sequence increases by 3 each time.

questions.prompt: A parent says something feels wrong, but the checklist looks normal. What is the safest response?
choices: Dismiss it because the checklist is normal
questions.explanation: Concerns should be heard and assessed alongside checks.

questions.prompt: Only verified details should guide final care update. A detail is useful but unverified. What should happen?
choices: Check it or describe it cautiously
questions.explanation: Useful but unverified information should be checked or framed cautiously.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports concern about change?
choices: A clear difference from the usual pattern
questions.explanation: A meaningful change from the usual pattern can matter.

questions.prompt: Two safe plans are possible. One is faster; one better matches the parent’s informed preference. What matters most?
choices: Balance safety, evidence, and informed preference
Choose the plan with fewer words
questions.explanation: Good care balances safety, evidence, and informed choice.
